title: Chapter 33: The Hearing
content: 500 Pearl Street. The Daniel Patrick Moynihan U.S. Courthouse. I've walked past this building a hundred times and never once thought I'd be walking in as a plaintiff against a literal demon corporation. Life is weird. Afterlife is weirder.

I clear security in the navy suit Evelyn picked out. She showed up at my apartment at six this morning with a garment bag and a look that could freeze hydrogen. "You're a plaintiff," she said, handing it to me. "Not a personality." Which is Evelyn-speak for: wear the boring suit, don't roll your eyes, let me do the talking. The suit fits like it was made for me, because of course it was — Evelyn had it tailored. She's been preparing for this longer than I've known she was preparing for this.

The courtroom is smaller than I expected. Way smaller. Movies make courtrooms look like cathedrals. This one's more like a rich person's study. Wood paneling everywhere — old, dark, oiled stuff that smells like a library and a church had a baby. An American flag stands to the left of the bench. The seal of the Southern District of New York hangs behind it. The ceiling is high but the space is intimate, designed so every word lands.

Judge Miriam Morrison is already seated. She was appointed in 2019, and according to Evelyn's research briefing — which she gave me in the car like it was a final exam I was going to be tested on — Morrison built her reputation on tech-law cases. Software patents. Data privacy. Algorithmic discrimination. She's rumored to be shortlisted for the Second Circuit. She's reading our filing when I walk in, and she doesn't look up.

Evelyn's already at the plaintiff's table. Her briefcase is open, her files are organized in a way that probably has its own indexing system. She glances at me, checks my suit, gives a barely perceptible nod. Approved.
The other side arrives.

A woman in a charcoal suit. Expensive shoes — I notice because Evelyn notices, and I've learned to read Evelyn's micro-expressions the way normal people read weather. The woman's shoes are Louboutins. Evelyn clocked them and filed the information. The woman's smile doesn't reach her eyes. Per the filing, she's listed as "Counsel for the Defendant." No personal name. Because of course Mephisto's legal representation wouldn't use a human name. Why would they? Names are for people.
She rises when the judge enters. We all rise. The ritual of it — the standing, the sitting, the "All rise" — it's theater. But it's theater with teeth.
Judge Morrison settles in. She has a calm, unhurried voice, the kind that makes you lean forward to hear it. "Counsel, I've reviewed the motions. Defendant's motion to dismiss first."

Charcoal Suit stands. She speaks in perfect legal cadence — measured, confident, every term precision-tooled. "Your Honor, the plaintiff's claims are predicated on a jurisdictional framework the contract explicitly assigns to the Infernal Court. The Southern District lacks subject-matter jurisdiction. The parties agreed to binding arbitration under Infernal Law, and this court cannot override a valid forum-selection clause."
She sits down like she just delivered a checkmate.

Evelyn rises. She doesn't use notes. She has them — they're on the table — but she doesn't look at them. "Your Honor, the so-called Infernal Court is a fictitious forum that does not appear in any directory of recognized judicial bodies, domestic or international. A contract cannot create jurisdiction where none exists. The plaintiff is a U.S. citizen, the harm occurred on U.S. soil, and the defendant operates through U.S. digital infrastructure. This court has jurisdiction."

Her voice is steady. Practiced. This is the part she's good at — the part where she takes something absurd and makes it sound like the most reasonable argument ever presented. And the thing is, she's right. A contract can't conjure a court into existence. You can't just write "all disputes shall be resolved by the Galactic Senate" and expect a federal judge to say, "Well, you both signed it."

Judge Morrison leans back. She's quiet for a moment. The rustle of legal documents fills the silence — the court reporter's fingers on the stenotype, the air system humming, the creak of the judge's chair.
Then she asks the question.
"Counsel, I've read the allegations." She pauses. "They're... extraordinary. Are you telling me that a mobile application can extract human emotions?"

The room holds its breath. This is the moment. This is where every supernatural case in every movie falls apart — the part where the judge laughs and throws it out. Where the skeptic wins and the believer goes home.
Evelyn doesn't flinch. "Yes, Your Honor. And I can prove it."

She presents the evidence. Leo's data visualizations — heat maps of emotional data, extraction timelines, the whole architecture laid out in color-coded clarity. Archive screenshots from the app's backend, the extraction protocols, the deduction records with my name on them. She hands them up to the bench, page by page, exhibit by exhibit.

Judge Morrison examines each one. Her reading glasses slide down her nose. She turns pages slowly. Her expression shifts — not from skepticism to belief, that's too simple. It shifts from skepticism to something more complicated. Interest. Curiosity. The look of someone who came in expecting a circus and is now looking at a crime scene.
She looks at me.
"Ms. Hart. The plaintiff. Will you testify?"
I stand. The chair scrapes against the floor. "Yes, Your Honor."

The witness stand is cold. Not metaphorically — the wood is genuinely cold under my palms, like the air conditioning is directed specifically at this spot. Maybe it is. Maybe they keep witnesses uncomfortable on purpose. I sit. I place my hands flat on the surface. The cold seeps through my fingers.
Evelyn approaches. She doesn't lead — she asks. Precise. Clinical. She walks me through the deductions. When they happened. Which emotions. What the app took.
I answer in the same register. Clinical. Precise. Which is the whole point. A person who's had her emotions stolen shouldn't be able to emote about having them stolen. The absence is the evidence.

"Your Honor, I am unable to feel empathy, trust, grief, nostalgia, or love as a direct result of the defendant's data extraction." I can hear my own voice — flat, even, like I'm reading a grocery list. "I can describe what each feeling was like because I remember the fact of having felt it. But the subjective experience — the warmth, the ache, the connection — has been removed. I am a person who knows she should feel things and cannot."
The courtroom is silent. No shuffling. No whispering. The kind of quiet that only happens when every person in a room is holding the same breath.
Judge Morrison looks at me for a long time. Then she asks: "Can you describe what it's like to know you should feel something and not be able to?"

And here's the thing about this question. She's not asking for the record. She's not asking as a legal formality. She's asking because she wants to know. Because something in what I said got past the robe and the bench and the years of judicial composure. She's asking as a person.
"It's like reading a menu in a language you used to speak," I say. "You recognize the words. You know what the dishes should taste like. But you can't order."
The silence after is physical. It has weight. I can feel it pressing against my skin. The court reporter's machine is quiet. Even the air system seems to hush.
Judge Morrison writes something. She doesn't look up for several seconds. When she does, she nods at Evelyn. "Redirect, counsel."
Evelyn shakes her head. "No further questions, Your Honor."
Charcoal Suit declines cross-examination. Smart. What's she going to do — prove I can feel things? Make me cry on command? There's nothing to cross. The emptiness speaks for itself.

I step down. The water fountain outside the courtroom tastes like metal and fluoride and every other public water fountain in every other government building in America. I drink it anyway. My mouth was dry and I didn't notice until now. That's the thing about being hollowed — you don't notice physical discomfort the way you used to, because the emotional alarm system is gone. Your body still feels. You just don't care as much that it does.
Evelyn's closing argument.

She stands. She doesn't look at me. She looks at the judge. Eye contact, sustained and deliberate, and I've seen Evelyn give a lot of arguments but I've never seen her like this. This isn't performance. This isn't strategy. This is my sister, who spent three years pretending this wasn't personal, finally letting it be personal in the only way she knows how — through the law.

"Your Honor, the plaintiff's contract was drafted under a legal framework that predates the internet, the smartphone, and the data economy. The defendant has operated with impunity because no one has challenged the foundation." She pauses. Let it land. "The foundation is rotten. It was built on law that no longer exists, enforced by mechanisms that violate current federal statute, and protected by a jurisdictional fiction. The plaintiff is asking this court to do what courts have always done: apply the law as it exists today to conduct that violates that law."

She takes a breath. The courtroom is so quiet I can hear my own heartbeat. Or maybe that's just my imagination. Maybe I can't actually feel my heartbeat and I'm remembering what it used to feel like to sense it.
"The fact that the defendant is... unconventional... does not place it above the law." Another pause. Longer this time. "Nothing is above the law. Not a corporation. Not a contract."
She doesn't pause before the last word. She lets it fall clean.
"Not even Hell."

It lands like a gavel. The courtroom doesn't gasp — courtrooms don't gasp, that's movies. But something shifts. The air pressure changes. The judge's pen stops moving. Charcoal Suit's smile has been gone for a while, but now even the memory of it is gone.

Judge Morrison is quiet for five full seconds. Then she speaks. "The court will take the motion under advisement. The parties will submit supplemental briefing within forty-eight hours. Ruling within one week."
She stands. We stand. She exits.
That's it. No ruling today. No dramatic verdict. No movie ending. Just time — a week of it — hanging in the air like a held breath.

I walk out of the courthouse and into a wall of cameras. Flash photography hits my retinas like tiny explosions. Reporters shout questions — "Ms. Hart, how did it feel to testify?" "Can you really not feel emotions?" "Is it true you're suing the devil?" — and I don't answer. Not because I'm being strategic. Because I genuinely don't know how to respond to "how did it feel." The irony is a cliche I don't have the bandwidth for.

I get in the car. Evelyn's car. She's already inside, typing on her phone, probably already drafting the supplemental brief. She doesn't look up when I close the door. She doesn't ask how I'm doing. She just drives.
I call Leo.
"How's the code?"

His voice is tired but focused. The sound of someone who's been staring at a screen for too long and is running on caffeine and spite. "The unDRM script is ready. I can deploy it through the audit gateway if you give me access."
"Do it tonight. While they're focused on the legal battle, we hit them from the technical side."
"If it works — and that's a big if — every hollowed creator will have their boot sequence overwritten. They'll start waking up with their emotions intact."
"And if it doesn't work?"
"Then we only have the legal side. Which might be enough."

I look out the window. New York scrolls by — glass towers and brownstones and streets that have seen every kind of war. Revolutionary. Civil. Cultural. Financial. Now this one. A war fought in courtrooms and codebases, in evidence exhibits and shell scripts. A war for the stuff that makes people people.
"It has to be both," I say. "Code and case law. One gets the emotions back. The other makes sure they can never be taken again."
Leo's quiet for a beat. "Tonight, then."
"Tonight."
I hang up. The city blurs past. One week until the ruling. Tonight, Leo deploys the code. Two fronts. One war.
And for the first time since this started, I think we might actually win.
